title: Olive oil and orange cake
subtitle: A moist, one-bowl Mediterranean cake that keeps for three days
excerpt: Olive oil replaces butter in this simple sponge, producing a cake that stays moist at room temperature for longer than a butter-based equivalent. Orange zest and juice run through the batter, and the finished cake is soaked with a thin orange syrup while still warm. Mixing takes about 10 minutes; the bake is 40 minutes.
sourceNotes: Olive oil cakes are traditional across the Mediterranean, from the Ligurian focaccia dolce to the Greek revani and the Catalan pa de pessic. The orange and olive oil combination is particularly common in Southern Italian and Sicilian baking, where both ingredients are staples. The recipe here follows the method documented in Giorgio Locatelli's 'Made in Italy' (2006) and in Yotam Ottolenghi's 'Jerusalem' (2012).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze without the syrup soak for up to 2 months. Add the syrup after thawing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The cake keeps for 3 days at room temperature wrapped. It improves on the second day once the syrup has fully absorbed.

glossaryTerms.definition: Using olive oil in place of butter in cake batter produces a different crumb structure. Butter is an emulsion of water and fat; when creamed with sugar it traps air and creates a light, springy crumb. Olive oil is pure fat with no water, so it does not cream. Instead it coats the gluten strands after they form, producing a denser, moister crumb. The cake keeps longer because there is no water content to evaporate. Use a mild or light olive oil rather than a peppery extra-virgin variety, which can give a bitter note in a sweet cake.

content.text: The method for this cake is different from a creamed butter sponge. Because

cannot be creamed, the eggs and sugar are whisked first to build the aeration, then the oil, flour and orange are folded in. This produces a cake that is denser than a Victoria sponge but stays moist for three days at room temperature.

Use unwaxed oranges, or scrub the skin thoroughly under hot water before zesting. The orange syrup that goes on while the cake is warm is optional but strongly recommended: it gives the crust a gloss and drives orange flavour into the crumb.

items.prepNote: zest of both, juice of both (about 150 ml)
juice only (about 75 ml), for the syrup

content.text: Heat the oven to 175 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 23 cm round cake tin. Whisk the 3 eggs and 180 g caster sugar together for 2 to 3 minutes until slightly pale and thickened. Whisk in the 150 ml olive oil, then the orange zest and juice. Sift in the 200 g flour and 1.5 tsp baking powder and fold in until smooth.
Pour into the prepared tin and bake for 38 to 45 minutes until the top is deep golden brown, the edges are pulling from the tin, and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. Leave in the tin.

While the cake bakes, stir the 50 g caster sugar into the 75 ml orange juice in a small pan over low heat until dissolved. Do not boil. Prick the warm cake all over with a skewer, then spoon the syrup over it slowly, letting each spoonful absorb before adding the next. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then turn out onto a rack.

items.problem: Cake is very dense and flat
items.solution: The eggs were not whisked long enough before adding the oil. They should be slightly pale and holding a faint trail from the whisk before the oil goes in. This aeration is the only lift the cake gets.
The oil used was a peppery extra-virgin variety. Use a mild or light olive oil labelled as such, or substitute a neutral vegetable oil if the olive flavour is not wanted.

items.problem: Syrup pools on the surface rather than absorbing
items.solution: The syrup was added all at once, or the prick holes were too shallow. Add it a spoonful at a time and wait 15 seconds between spoonfuls. If the cake has cooled completely, the syrup will absorb more slowly.
